APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses cross-lingual named entity recognition and linking for 282 languages, a topic squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues given its scale and practical relevance. It uses community-standard methods (BiLSTM+CRF, self-training, unsupervised linking via translation) and evaluation practices (F-score) and compares to appropriate baselines including state-of-the-art language-independent methods and supervised gold-standard models. Datasets and resources (Wikipedia, LORELEI, DBpedia/Freebase/YAGO) are those expected by the community, and the thorough related-work coverage and public code further support fit and audience relevance. | B(5): The work addresses cross-lingual named-entity recognition and linking for 282 Wikipedia languages, a core NLP problem of clear relevance to top-tier NLP/ML venues. It uses standard and expected methods (BiLSTM+CRF tagger, self-training, morphology features, translation-based linking) and evaluates on appropriate datasets (Wikipedia, DARPA LORELEI non-Wikipedia data) against state-of-the-art and supervised baselines using F-score. The scale (282 languages), thorough related work coverage, and code release further position it well for the venue’s audience and standards.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses cross-lingual named entity recognition and linking for 282 languages, a topic squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues given its scale and practical relevance. It uses community-standard methods (BiLSTM+CRF, self-training, unsupervised linking via translation) and evaluation practices (F-score) and compares to appropriate baselines including state-of-the-art language-independent methods and supervised gold-standard models. Datasets and resources (Wikipedia, LORELEI, DBpedia/Freebase/YAGO) are those expected by the community, and the thorough related-work coverage and public code further support fit and audience relevance.

reviewer_b.justification: The work addresses cross-lingual named-entity recognition and linking for 282 Wikipedia languages, a core NLP problem of clear relevance to top-tier NLP/ML venues. It uses standard and expected methods (BiLSTM+CRF tagger, self-training, morphology features, translation-based linking) and evaluates on appropriate datasets (Wikipedia, DARPA LORELEI non-Wikipedia data) against state-of-the-art and supervised baselines using F-score. The scale (282 languages), thorough related work coverage, and code release further position it well for the venue’s audience and standards.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of cross-lingual name tagging and linking at massive scale (282 Wikipedia languages) and provides practical techniques (silver-standard annotation transfer, self-training, morphology features, translation mining) that yield substantial gains (e.g., 6%–12% F-score from self-training; 7–11% from morphology) and comparable performance to supervised models on non-Wikipedia data. By producing code and a wide-coverage multilingual resource linked to an English KB (DBpedia), the work is likely to be adopted and built upon by researchers and practitioners working on low-resource languages. Its limitations (incomplete silver annotations, lower F-scores vs large manually annotated datasets) constrain ultimate performance but do not negate the significant practical and research value of enabling broad cross-lingual entity processing. | B(4): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—scalable cross-lingual name tagging and linking—by producing silver-standard annotations and models for 282 Wikipedia languages and releasing code. Empirical outcomes (comparable performance to supervised models on non-Wikipedia data, large self-training gains of ~6% on Wikipedia and ~12% off-Wikipedia in Turkish, and morphology feature gains of 11.1% for Turkish and 7.1% for Uzbek) indicate the approach can materially improve NER/EL for low-resource languages and influence practitioner choices. Notable limitations (incomplete silver labels and being 5.8%–17.6% worse than large gold-trained models) prevent it from being fully transformative, but it still opens significant follow-on research and is likely to be built upon widely.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of cross-lingual name tagging and linking at massive scale (282 Wikipedia languages) and provides practical techniques (silver-standard annotation transfer, self-training, morphology features, translation mining) that yield substantial gains (e.g., 6%–12% F-score from self-training; 7–11% from morphology) and comparable performance to supervised models on non-Wikipedia data. By producing code and a wide-coverage multilingual resource linked to an English KB (DBpedia), the work is likely to be adopted and built upon by researchers and practitioners working on low-resource languages. Its limitations (incomplete silver annotations, lower F-scores vs large manually annotated datasets) constrain ultimate performance but do not negate the significant practical and research value of enabling broad cross-lingual entity processing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—scalable cross-lingual name tagging and linking—by producing silver-standard annotations and models for 282 Wikipedia languages and releasing code. Empirical outcomes (comparable performance to supervised models on non-Wikipedia data, large self-training gains of ~6% on Wikipedia and ~12% off-Wikipedia in Turkish, and morphology feature gains of 11.1% for Turkish and 7.1% for Uzbek) indicate the approach can materially improve NER/EL for low-resource languages and influence practitioner choices. Notable limitations (incomplete silver labels and being 5.8%–17.6% worse than large gold-trained models) prevent it from being fully transformative, but it still opens significant follow-on research and is likely to be built upon widely.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experiments across 282 Wikipedia languages and non-Wikipedia corpora, with concrete quantitative gains reported for self-training and morphology features and comparisons to state-of-the-art and supervised baselines. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears generally sound (multiple datasets, reasonable baselines), but important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests) and the approach relies on incomplete silver-standard Wikipedia annotations (which the authors acknowledge), creating potential evaluation bias. These are minor issues that do not appear to invalidate the primary technical conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments on Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia data with comparisons to state-of-the-art baselines and supervised models are reported, and quantitative gains for self-training (6% on Wikipedia, 12% on non-Wikipedia) and morphology features (11.1% Turkish, 7.1% Uzbek) are provided. No formal mathematical derivations were needed or presented. The experimental setup is largely sound (appropriate datasets and baselines), but some methodological details are missing (hyperparameters were not reported and no statistical significance testing is mentioned), and the authors acknowledge known limitations of the silver-standard data. These are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by experiments across 282 Wikipedia languages and non-Wikipedia corpora, with concrete quantitative gains reported for self-training and morphology features and comparisons to state-of-the-art and supervised baselines. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears generally sound (multiple datasets, reasonable baselines), but important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests) and the approach relies on incomplete silver-standard Wikipedia annotations (which the authors acknowledge), creating potential evaluation bias. These are minor issues that do not appear to invalidate the primary technical conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments on Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia data with comparisons to state-of-the-art baselines and supervised models are reported, and quantitative gains for self-training (6% on Wikipedia, 12% on non-Wikipedia) and morphology features (11.1% Turkish, 7.1% Uzbek) are provided. No formal mathematical derivations were needed or presented. The experimental setup is largely sound (appropriate datasets and baselines), but some methodological details are missing (hyperparameters were not reported and no statistical significance testing is mentioned), and the authors acknowledge known limitations of the silver-standard data. These are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs broad experiments (282 Wikipedia languages plus non-Wikipedia LORELEI/news/forum data and multiple KBs) and compares to both language-independent baselines and supervised models, supporting the main claims. Ablation-style analyses are presented for self-training and morphology features (with concrete F-score gains for Turkish and Uzbek) and limitations are explicitly discussed. Related work is described as thorough and code is available. However, missing hyperparameter reporting and possible gaps in the breadth of ablation/sensitivity analyses are minor reproducibility and analysis shortcomings. | B(4): The experiments are broad and convincing: evaluations cover 282 Wikipedia languages, additional non-Wikipedia news/forum datasets, and comparisons to language-independent baselines and supervised models. Ablation-style analyses are provided for key components (self-training and morphology), with quantified gains (e.g., self-training: +6% on Wikipedia, +12% on non-Wikipedia in Turkish; morphology: +11.1% Turkish, +7.1% Uzbek). Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations are openly discussed (incomplete silver labels, morphology assumptions, 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores vs. large supervised datasets). Reproducibility is partially supported (code available) but weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and the inherent noise/incompleteness of the silver-standard annotations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs broad experiments (282 Wikipedia languages plus non-Wikipedia LORELEI/news/forum data and multiple KBs) and compares to both language-independent baselines and supervised models, supporting the main claims. Ablation-style analyses are presented for self-training and morphology features (with concrete F-score gains for Turkish and Uzbek) and limitations are explicitly discussed. Related work is described as thorough and code is available. However, missing hyperparameter reporting and possible gaps in the breadth of ablation/sensitivity analyses are minor reproducibility and analysis shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad and convincing: evaluations cover 282 Wikipedia languages, additional non-Wikipedia news/forum datasets, and comparisons to language-independent baselines and supervised models. Ablation-style analyses are provided for key components (self-training and morphology), with quantified gains (e.g., self-training: +6% on Wikipedia, +12% on non-Wikipedia in Turkish; morphology: +11.1% Turkish, +7.1% Uzbek). Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations are openly discussed (incomplete silver labels, morphology assumptions, 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores vs. large supervised datasets). Reproducibility is partially supported (code available) but weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and the inherent noise/incompleteness of the silver-standard annotations.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from methodology (e.g., BiLSTM+CRF tagger, self-training, morphology features, translation/pair mining) to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (cross-lingual framework for 282 languages, self-training gains, morphology feature gains) are stated early. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, which supports clarity; figures/tables/notation are not highlighted in the summary. Minor presentation shortcomings remain (important experimental details such as hyperparameter reporting are missing and some dataset/annotation limitations are only partially addressed), so overall clarity is strong but not exceptional. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical method→experiments→conclusion flow and clearly states its main contributions early (cross-lingual tagging/linking for 282 languages, silver-standard annotation pipeline, self-training and morphology/translation enhancements). Notation and methodology (BiLSTM+CRF, self-training, morphology features, translation-based linking) are described clearly and related work coverage is thorough; experimental results and specific gains (e.g., 6%/12% self-training, 11.1%/7.1% morphology gains) are reported, implying effective figures/tables. The chief presentation shortcoming is omission of hyperparameter details, which hinders reproducibility and is a minor clarity gap. Overall writing quality is good with only minor issues to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from methodology (e.g., BiLSTM+CRF tagger, self-training, morphology features, translation/pair mining) to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (cross-lingual framework for 282 languages, self-training gains, morphology feature gains) are stated early. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, which supports clarity; figures/tables/notation are not highlighted in the summary. Minor presentation shortcomings remain (important experimental details such as hyperparameter reporting are missing and some dataset/annotation limitations are only partially addressed), so overall clarity is strong but not exceptional.

Notation and methodology (BiLSTM+CRF, self-training, morphology features, translation-based linking) are described clearly and related work coverage is thorough; experimental results and specific gains (e.g., 6%/12% self-training, 11.1%/7.1% morphology gains) are reported, implying effective figures/tables. The chief presentation shortcoming is omission of hyperparameter details, which hinders reproducibility and is a minor clarity gap. Overall writing quality is good with only minor issues to address.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (cross-lingual NER/EL) and primarily composes known techniques (Wikipedia distant supervision, BiLSTM-CRF tagging, self-training, translation via title pairs, and unsupervised linking) at large scale. Novelty lies in the particular combination and engineering refinements — e.g., anchor-link-derived morphology features, topic selection for self-training, and scaling the pipeline to 282 languages — but these are incremental advances rather than fundamentally new methods. The work is a meaningful, practical contribution that would inform experts about scalability and specific refinements, but it does not introduce a genuinely new paradigm.  | B(3): The paper largely combines established resources and techniques (Wikipedia-derived silver annotations, cross-lingual title pairs, translation-based linking) but applies them at an unusually large scale (282 languages) and introduces non-trivial extensions such as anchor-link-derived morphology features, self-training with topic selection, and mined word-translation pairs. These extensions yield measurable gains (e.g., 6%–12% self-training improvements; 7–11% morphology gains) and are clearly differentiated from prior work, but they are incremental methodological advances and engineering at scale rather than a fundamentally new idea or problem formulation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (cross-lingual NER/EL) and primarily composes known techniques (Wikipedia distant supervision, BiLSTM-CRF tagging, self-training, translation via title pairs, and unsupervised linking) at large scale. Novelty lies in the particular combination and engineering refinements — e.g., anchor-link-derived morphology features, topic selection for self-training, and scaling the pipeline to 282 languages — but these are incremental advances rather than fundamentally new methods. The work is a meaningful, practical contribution that would inform experts about scalability and specific refinements, but it does not introduce a genuinely new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper largely combines established resources and techniques (Wikipedia-derived silver annotations, cross-lingual title pairs, translation-based linking) but applies them at an unusually large scale (282 languages) and introduces non-trivial extensions such as anchor-link-derived morphology features, self-training with topic selection, and mined word-translation pairs. These extensions yield measurable gains (e.g., 6%–12% self-training improvements; 7–11% morphology gains) and are clearly differentiated from prior work, but they are incremental methodological advances and engineering at scale rather than a fundamentally new idea or problem formulation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against state-of-the-art language-independent baselines (Zhang et al., 2016a; Tsai et al., 2016) and supervised models, evaluates on the same data types (Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia LORELEI) using F-score, and quantifies and acknowledges when prior supervised methods perform better (5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores). It also analyzes differences and component contributions (self-training and morphology gains, Turkish/Uzbek case studies) and reports thorough related-work coverage. A minor shortcoming for full fairness/reproducibility is that hyperparameters were not reported, so comparisons are strong but not perfectly comprehensive. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate recent baselines (state-of-the-art language-independent methods such as Zhang et al. 2016a and Tsai et al. 2016, and supervised models trained on manually annotated data). Comparisons use the same data sources (Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia corpora) and F-score metrics, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when supervised prior models perform better (reporting 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores). Differences are analyzed quantitatively (e.g., ablations showing self-training and morphology feature gains), and related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against state-of-the-art language-independent baselines (Zhang et al., 2016a; Tsai et al., 2016) and supervised models, evaluates on the same data types (Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia LORELEI) using F-score, and quantifies and acknowledges when prior supervised methods perform better (5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores). It also analyzes differences and component contributions (self-training and morphology gains, Turkish/Uzbek case studies) and reports thorough related-work coverage. A minor shortcoming for full fairness/reproducibility is that hyperparameters were not reported, so comparisons are strong but not perfectly comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate recent baselines (state-of-the-art language-independent methods such as Zhang et al. 2016a and Tsai et al. 2016, and supervised models trained on manually annotated data). Comparisons use the same data sources (Wikipedia and non-Wikipedia corpora) and F-score metrics, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when supervised prior models perform better (reporting 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores). Differences are analyzed quantitatively (e.g., ablations showing self-training and morphology feature gains), and related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

main_claims: A cross-lingual name tagging and linking framework was developed for 282 Wikipedia languages that can identify name mentions, assign coarse- or fine-grained types, and link linkable mentions to an English KB (DBpedia).

The framework generates silver-standard annotations by transferring English annotations via cross-lingual links and KB properties, refines them via self-training and topic selection, derives language-specific morphology features from anchor links, and mines word translation pairs to enable cross-lingual linking.

On Wikipedia data the name tagging and linking results are reported as promising for 282 languages, and on non-Wikipedia data (news and forums) the approach is comparable to supervised models trained from manually annotated documents; however it attains 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores than models trained on large manually annotated gold-standard data.

Self-training and derived morphology features yield substantial improvements: self-training gave a 6% absolute F-score gain on Wikipedia and 12% on non-Wikipedia (Turkish case study); morphology features gave 11.1% and 7.1% absolute F-score gains for Turkish and Uzbek respectively.
_summary.methodology: Generate silver-standard name annotations from Wikipedia markups and cross-lingual links, train a Bi-directional LSTM + CRF name tagger, apply self-training over clustered unlabeled sentences, derive morphology (affix/stem) features from anchor links, mine word translation pairs from cross-lingual title pairs, translate mentions to English and perform unsupervised collective inference to link translated mentions to an English KB (DBpedia).
datasets: DARPA LORELEI (Leidos) corpus / Non-Wikipedia news and discussion forum data
baselines: State-of-the-art language-independent methods (Zhang et al., 2016a; Tsai et al., 2016)
Models trained from manually annotated gold-standard documents (supervised models)
limitations: Silver-standard annotations derived from Wikipedia are incomplete (e.g., only the first mention in an article is often labeled).
The morphology analysis from anchor-link-derived affix lists only works when stems and affixes are directly concatenated.
The approach achieves 5.8%–17.6% lower F-scores than models trained on large manually annotated gold-standard datasets for non-Wikipedia data.
Only a small percentage of name mentions can be covered by some approaches (as noted in comparison to prior work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - cross-lingual named entity recognition and entity linking
